entries.title: Integer division
entries.blurb: Compute m / n: quotient in d, remainder in r.
entries.theory:
Computes **m / n** by repeated subtraction. After termination: `d = m // n` and `r = m mod n` with `0 <= r < n`.

This is the running example for almost every chapter — syntax, semantics, complexity, and Hoare logic.
entries.post: d * n + r == m and 0 <= r and r < n

entries.title: Search for quadratic root
entries.blurb: Find a non-negative integer root of a quadratic by linear search.
entries.theory:
Searches `x = 0, 1, 2, ...` and stops at the first value satisfying `a*x^2 + b*x + c = 0` **or** `a*x^2 - b*x + c = 0`. Diverges if no integer root exists.

For `(a,b,c) = (1, -5, 6)` the polynomial `x^2 - 5x + 6` has roots `2` and `3`; the search halts at `x = 2`.

entries.title: GCD by subtraction (Euclid's first algorithm)
entries.blurb: Repeatedly subtract the smaller from the larger until they are equal.
entries.theory:
Subtracts the smaller of `x, y` from the larger until they coincide. The common value is `gcd(m, n)`.

This is the **inefficient** version (linear in `max(m, n)`); contrast with Exercise 10 which uses the modulo trick.

entries.title: Primality test by trial division
entries.blurb: z = 1 if n is prime, 0 otherwise.
entries.theory:
Tries every divisor `y = n-1, n-2, ..., 2` and divides `n` by `y` (using the inner subtraction loop) to find the remainder. If any remainder is `0`, sets `z := 0`.

For `n = 1` the outer loop guard `2 <= y` is already false, so `z` stays at `1`.

entries.title: Integer square root
entries.blurb: z = floor(sqrt(x)), r = x - z^2.
entries.theory: Increments `z` while `(z+1)^2 <= x`. After termination: `z^2 <= x < (z+1)^2`, so `z = floor(sqrt(x))`, and `r = x - z^2` is the rounding error.
entries.post: z * z <= x and x < (z + 1) * (z + 1) and r == x - z * z

entries.title: Fibonacci
entries.blurb: After x iterations: y = F(x+1), z = F(x).
entries.theory:
Three-variable rolling Fibonacci: invariant `(z, y) = (F(k), F(k+1))` after `k = (initial x) - x` iterations. Final `y = F(x_0 + 1)`, `z = F(x_0)`.

Note `t` is just a temp; the rotation is the same as `(z, y) := (y, z + y)`.
entries.post: y >= 1 and z >= 0 and z <= y

entries.title: Simulate repeat-until
entries.blurb: A While encoding of `repeat S until B` for the body `x := x + 1` and guard `x = 5`.
entries.theory: While doesn't have a built-in repeat-until, but you can simulate it by **executing the body once before** the while loop. This program models `repeat x := x + 1 until x = 5`.

entries.title: Branch without using `if` (Ch1 style)
entries.blurb: Set y = 1 if x = 0, otherwise y = 2 — using only while.
entries.theory:
Encoding a two-way branch with two while loops and a `done` flag. The first loop fires when `x = 0`; the second loop fires when `x != 0`. The flag prevents both from running.

Useful for proving you don't *need* `if` syntactically — but for readability you obviously do.

entries.title: Parity check
entries.blurb: `is_odd = 1` iff n is odd.
entries.theory: Decrements `|n|` by 1 each iteration and toggles `is_odd in {0, 1}`. Final value: `1` if `n` is odd, `0` if even. Handles negative `n` by absolute-value normalising first.

entries.title: Count cubes in [m, n]
entries.blurb: Count integers i with m <= i^3 <= n.
entries.theory:
Iterates `i = 0, 1, 2, ...` while `i^3 <= n` and counts those whose cube also satisfies `m <= i^3`.

Note: counts only **non-negative** `i`, so for negative-cube targets the count will exclude them.
entries.post: count >= 0 and i * i * i > n

entries.title: Linear Diophantine — solve m*x + n*y = k
entries.blurb: The chapter's clever diagonal search with a swap, not naive enumeration.
entries.theory:
Searches `(x, y)` with **diagonal walk + swap**: outer loop grows `x`, inner loop tries each `y <= x`. When `m*y + n*x = k` (note the swapped roles), the program **swaps `x` and `y`** so the result is the matching pair.

For `(m, n, k) = (3, 4, 7)` it terminates with `(x, y) = (1, 1)` since `3*1 + 4*1 = 7`. Diverges if no non-negative solution exists.

This is the canonical version from Chapter 1 of the notebook.

entries.title: Euclid's GCD via mod
entries.blurb: Faster than the subtraction version: each outer step does a remainder.
entries.theory: Standard `gcd(m, n) = gcd(n, m mod n)` recursion, expressed as a while loop. The inner loop computes `r = x mod y` by repeated subtraction. Logarithmic in `max(m, n)`.

entries.title: Extra 1 — Multiplication by repeated addition
entries.blurb: A standard exercise: compute m*n in z without using `*` on the outer body.
entries.theory: Loop invariant: `z + y*n = m*n` (i.e. partial product plus what is left to add). Decrements `y` by 1 each iteration.

entries.title: Extra 2 — Power m^n by repeated multiplication
entries.blurb: Same shape as multiplication, but `+` becomes `*` and the seed is `1`.
entries.theory:
Loop invariant: `x * m^y = m^n` (partial power times what is left). Decrements `y` by 1.

This is the same skeleton as Chapter 4 Exercise 32 (the chapter does the Hoare proof for it).

entries.title: Diverges: while 0 <= x do x := x + 1
entries.blurb: Doesn't terminate from x = 0 — useful for non-termination intuition.
entries.theory: Trivially diverges: every step increments `x`, the guard `0 <= x` never becomes false. Run **Count steps** with a small step budget to see how far it gets.

entries.title: Quadratic root search (semantics walkthrough)
entries.blurb: Same program as Example 2; Chapter 2 walks its small-step trace.
entries.theory: Use **Trace** to see every reduction step. Use **State trace** to see only how variables change.

entries.title: Step-count integer division (semantics)
entries.blurb: Same div_prog as Example 1, asked from a semantics angle.
entries.theory: Try **Count steps** and **State trace** to see how many small-step rules each iteration consumes.
entries.post: d * n + r == m and 0 <= r and r < n

entries.title: GCD subtraction (semantics)
entries.blurb: Same gcd_prog as Example 3, walked through with the SOS rules.
entries.theory: Look at the **Loop snapshots** view to see only the points where the while guard is re-evaluated.

entries.title: Diophantine (semantics walkthrough)
entries.blurb: Same diophantine program as Exercise 7, this time as a semantics example.
entries.theory: A nested loop is a great stress-test for the SOS rules — try **State trace** then **Loop snapshots** to see how the snapshot view filters the noise.

entries.title: Branch without `if` (variant: 100 / 200)
entries.blurb: Same shape as Exercise 4 but with concrete y values 100 and 200.
entries.theory: Two-loop encoding of a two-way branch. The semantics chapter uses this to show that two distinct flag states give two distinct final states.

entries.title: Extra 1 — Multiplication, alternative form
entries.blurb: Same task as Ch1 Extra 1, written with a count-down counter.
entries.theory: Equivalent to "multiplication by repeated addition" but uses `i := n` and decrements while `1 <= i`. Try **State trace** to see the two are equivalent under semantics.

entries.title: Tiny step-count exercise
entries.blurb: Quiz program from the notes — count its steps.
entries.theory: A short program: an assignment, an `if`, and a 2-iteration `while`. Step count is independent of inputs (constant) — try **Count steps** to verify.

entries.title: Halve x = i*(i+1) by 2 each step
entries.blurb: Step count is Theta(i^2) because x ~= i^2.
entries.theory: Initial `x = i*(i+1)`, each iteration does `x := x - 2`. Iterations: about `i*(i+1)/2`. Step count: **Theta(i^2)**. Use **Count steps** at `i = 5, 10, 20` to see the quadratic growth.

entries.title: Primality (counting steps)
entries.blurb: Same primality program as Example 4, asked from a complexity angle.
entries.theory: Outer loop runs `n-2` times. Inner loop is `Theta(n/y)`. Total: `Theta(n * log n)` in the worst case (composite). Try `n = 13` (prime) vs `n = 12` (composite).

entries.title: Triangular sum 1..n nested in 1..i
entries.blurb: Two-deep nested loop computing sum_i sum_j j.
entries.theory: Outer index `i` from 1 to `n`, inner from 1 to `i`. Step count is `Theta(n^2)`. Try **Count steps** at `n = 5, 10`.

entries.title: Three-loop cubic counter
entries.blurb: Triple-nested loops driven by `n` — Theta(n^3) total work.
entries.theory: Three concentric while loops, each running approximately `n` times. Total iterations: `Theta(n^3)`. **Note**: the loops mutate `n` itself; trace it with **State trace** to see.

entries.title: Extra 1 — Linear Theta(n)
entries.blurb: Single while loop counting from 0 to n.
entries.theory: Step count: **Theta(n)**. The simplest non-constant program in this language.

entries.title: Extra 2 — Quadratic Theta(n^2)
entries.blurb: Two nested loops of length n.
entries.theory: Step count: **Theta(n^2)**. Compare against Extra 1 by running **Count steps** at the same `n`.

entries.title: Extra 3 — Constant Theta(1)
entries.blurb: A single assignment — step count independent of state.
entries.theory: Step count: **Theta(1)** = a few small-step rule applications, regardless of `n`.

entries.title: Bijection N -> Z (beta function)
entries.blurb: x = 2n if n >= 0, else x = -2n - 1. The standard N <-> Z encoding.
entries.theory: If `n >= 0` then `x = 2n` (the evens enumerate the non-negatives). If `n < 0` then `x = -2n - 1` (the odds enumerate the negatives). This is the **beta** bijection used heavily in the computability chapter.
entries.post: (n >= 0 and x == 2 * n) or (n < 0 and x == -2 * n - 1)

entries.title: Power m^n (partial correctness)
entries.blurb: x = m^n on termination. Same as Ch1 Extra 2.
entries.theory: Loop invariant: `x * m^y = m^n` (partial power times what is left). Termination requires `n >= 0`.

entries.title: Sum 1..n
entries.blurb: x = n*(n+1)/2 on termination.
entries.theory: Classic Hoare-logic example. Loop invariant: `x = (i-1)*i/2 and i <= n+1`. Termination variant: `n + 1 - i`.

entries.title: Integer log base 2
entries.blurb: z = floor(log2(n)).
entries.theory: Doubles `x` (powers of 2) while `2x <= n`, counting in `z`. Final `z = floor(log2(n))` for `n >= 1`.

entries.title: Last digit of n divisible by 3?
entries.theory: Strips off all but the last digit (subtract 10 until below 10), then checks divisibility by 3 (subtract 3 until below 3). Sets `z := 1` iff the leftover is 0.

entries.title: Extra 1 — Factorial n!
entries.blurb: Same loop shape as power; the invariant is `x * y! = n!`.
entries.theory: Termination: `y` decreases by 1 each step. Invariant: `x * y! = n!`. Final: `x = n!`.

entries.title: "Even after 2n + 1" — always sets x = 0
entries.blurb: 2n + 1 is always odd, so this program always sets x := 0.
entries.theory: Sets `x := 2n + 1`, copies to `n`, halves (i.e., reduces mod 2) by repeated `n := n - 2`, then checks parity. Since `2n + 1` is always odd, the leftover is `1`, so the program sets `x := 0` (the test `n = 0` fails). A nice example for computable-function discussion.

entries.title: Conditional copy (prog_a)
entries.blurb: x = n if n > 100, else x = y. Used in the universal-program section.
entries.theory: If `n > 100` (i.e. `100 <= n` and `n != 100`) the program copies `n` to `x`. Otherwise it copies `y`. A toy example used for discussing computable functions.
entries.post: (n > 100 and x == n) or (n <= 100 and x == y)

entries.title: Diophantine (big-step example)
entries.blurb: The same swap-search Diophantine, used as the big-step example.
entries.theory: Big-step rules collapse a whole derivation into a single inference of the form `<S, sigma> -> sigma'`. Use **Trace** (small-step) and compare to the conceptually flatter big-step view.

entries.title: GCD by subtraction (big-step)
entries.blurb: Same gcd_prog used as a big-step worked example.
entries.theory: A short program — small-step and big-step derivation trees have very different shapes. The big-step proof is **finite-depth in the loop count**, not in the rule count.

entries.title: Halve x = i*(i+1) (big-step proof depth)
entries.blurb: Same as Ch3 Exercise 19; appendix uses it for proof-tree depth.
entries.theory: The big-step proof tree has one inference rule per loop iteration — depth is `Theta(i^2)`.

entries.title: GCD with deep big-step derivation
entries.blurb: Compute the depth of the big-step proof tree for gcd(12, 30).
entries.theory: The appendix walks through the proof depth of the GCD program. Run with the canonical `(m, n) = (12, 30)` then count loop iterations under **Loop snapshots**.

entries.title: Iterative Fibonacci (non-destructive)
entries.blurb: Same as Example 6 but written with explicit (a, b) rotation.
entries.theory: Standard two-variable Fibonacci. After `n` iterations: `a = F(n)`, `b = F(n + 1)`.
entries.post: a >= 0 and b >= 1 and b >= a

entries.title: Collatz conjecture
entries.blurb: Halves if even, else 3n + 1 — counts how many steps to reach 1.
entries.theory:
Famous open problem: this program is conjectured to **always terminate** for any positive `n`, but no proof exists. Try `n = 27` (it takes 111 steps).

Note: parity is computed via repeated subtraction since While has no `mod`.

entries.title: Infinite loop (deliberately diverges)
entries.blurb: while 0 <= x do x := x + 1 — useful for non-termination demos.
entries.theory: Identical to Example 9. Run **Count steps** with the default budget — the engine will stop and report 'step budget exhausted'.
